s, and our neighbors. Research by the Centers for Disease Control and

; one in four was beaten by a parent to the point of a mark being left on

of

As human beings we belong to an extremely resilient species. Since time
i

esilient species. Since time
immemorial we have rebounded from our relentless wars, countless disasters
(both natural and man-made), and the violence and betrayal in our own lives.

and betrayal in our own lives.
But traumatic experiences do leave traces, whether on a large scale (on our

n our families, with dark secrets being
imperceptibly passed down through generations. They also leave traces on our

ty for joy and intimacy, and even on our

Trauma affects not only those who are directly exposed to it, but also those
around them. Soldiers returning ho

nd them. Soldiers returning home from combat may frighten their families

tend to become depressed, and the children of depressed mothers are at

e as a child often makes it difficult to establish stable, trusting
relationships as an adult.
Trauma, by definition, is unbearable and intolerable. Most rape victims,
combat soldi

ost rape victims,
combat soldiers, and children who have been molested become so upset when

they think about what they experienced that they try to push it out of their minds,
try

ush it out of their minds,
trying to act as if nothing happened, and move on. It takes tremendous energy to
keep functioning while carrying the memory of terror, and the shame of u

of terror, and the shame of utter
weakness and vulnerability.
While we all want to move beyond trauma, the part of our brain that is
devoted to ensuring our survival (deep below o

ing our survival (deep below our rational brain) is not very good
at denial. Long after a traumatic experience is over, it may be reactivated at the
slightest hint of danger and mo

lightest hint of danger and mobilize disturbed brain circuits and secrete massive
amounts of stress hormones. This precipitates unpleasant emotions intense
physical sensations, and

tense
physical sensations, and impulsive and aggressive actions. These posttraumatic

control,
survivors of trauma often begin to fear that they are damaged to the core and
beyond redemption.

The first time I remember being drawn to study medicine was at a sum

to study medicine was at a summer camp

idneys work, how they secrete the body’s

I was riveted by his account of the miraculous way the bod

of the miraculous way the body functions. Later,
during every stage of my medical training, whether I was studying surgery,
cardiology, or pediatrics, it was obvious to me that th

, it was obvious to me that the key to healing was
understanding how the human organism works. When I began my psychiatry
rotation, however, I was struck by the contrast between th

uck by the contrast between the incredible
complexity of the mind and the ways that we human beings are connected and

little psychiatrists knew about the origins of the
problems they were treating. Would it be possible one day to know as much
about brains, minds, and love as we do about the other

love as we do about the other systems that make up our

We are obviously still years from attaining that sort of detailed
understanding, but the birth of three new branch

the birth of three new branches of science has led to an
explosion of knowledge about the effects of psychological trauma, abuse, and
neglect. Those new disciplines are neuroscien

new disciplines are neuroscience, the study of how the brain
supports mental processes; developmental psychopathology, the study of the

rse experiences on the development of mind and brain; and
interpersonal neurobiology, the study of how our behavior influences the

and mind-sets of those around us.
Research from these new disciplines has revealed that trauma produces
actual physiological changes, including a recalibration of the brain’s alar

alibration of the brain’s alarm
system, an increase in stress hormone activity, and alterations in the system that
filters relevant information from irrelevant. We now know that tr

rrelevant. We now know that trauma
compromises the brain area that communicates the physical, embodied feeling of
being alive. These changes explain why traumatized individuals bec

hy traumatized individuals become

day lives. They also help us understand why traumatized people s

stand why traumatized people so often keep
repeating the same problems and have such trouble learning from experience.
We now know that their behaviors are not the result of moral

s are not the result of moral failings or signs of
lack of willpower or bad character—they are caused by actual changes in the
brain.
This vast increase in our knowledge about the

se in our knowledge about the basic processes that underlie
trauma has also opened up new possibilities to palliate or even reverse the
damage. We can now develop methods and exper

now develop methods and experiences that utilize the brain’s

n with their lives. There are fundamentally three avenues: 1) top down,

nderstand what is going on with us, while processing the memories of the

or
by utilizing other technologies that change the way the brain organizes

deeply and viscerally contra

t
deeply and viscerally contradict the helplessness, rage, or collapse that result

is an

my
colleagues and students at the Trauma Center, which I founded thirty years ago.

lts: victims

icacy of many different forms of treatment, from medications to talking, yoga,

THE BURDEN OF TOXICITY
Exiles

THE BURDEN OF TOXICITY
Exiles are the toxic waste dump of the system. Because they hold the memories,
sensations, beliefs, and emotions associated with trauma, it is hazardous to

th trauma, it is hazardous to
release them. They contain the “Oh, my God, I’m done for” experience—the
essence of inescapable shock—and with it, terror, collapse, and accommodation

r, collapse, and accommodation.
Exiles may reveal themselves in the form of crushing physical sensations or
extreme numbing, and they offend both the reasonableness of the managers

reasonableness of the managers and
the bravado of the firefighters.
Like most incest survivors, Joan hated her exiles, particularly the little girl
who had responded to her abuser’

o had responded to her abuser’s sexual demands and the terrified child who
whimpered alone in her bed. When exiles overwhelm managers, they take us
over—we are nothing but that rej

er—we are nothing but that rejected, weak, unloved, and abandoned child.
The Self becomes “blended” with the exiles, and every possible alternative for
our life is eclipsed. Then,

r

rts that hold them—the parts that were hurt the most by

ving, lively, playful and innocent parts. By exiling them

ir original injury.”18 As Joan discovered, keeping the exiles hidden
and despised was condemning her to a life without intimacy or genuine joy.
